Space Exploration Technologies Corporation (SpaceX) is an El Segundo, California [[space transportation]] [[start-up]] company that is working to improve the cost and reliability of access to space ultimately by a factor of ten.

SpaceX has announced development of the Falcon family of mostly reusable two stage, RP-1 kerosene/liquid oxygen launch vehicles, including the [[Falcon I]] rocket with one lower stage engine and the larger [[Falcon V]] rocket with five lower stage engines.  The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for September, 2004, with a United States [[Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency]] (DARPA) [[Tacsat]] payload.  The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for late 2005 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002 by Chief Executive Officer Elon Musk.  Musk co-founded two previous start-up companies:  Zip2, a leader in the online news publishing software business; and [[PayPal]], a leader in the online funds transfer business.  Zip2 was sold in 1999 to the computer hardware and services company [[Compaq]] for US$341 million in cash and stock options [http://h18020.www1.hp.com/corporate/1999ar/nf/fin_notes2.html] and PayPal was sold in 2002 to online auctioneer [[eBay]] for US$1.5 billion in cash.  Musk funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales.

== External Links ==
[http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website]

Space Exploration Technologies Corporation ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to space "ultimately by a factor of ten".

SpaceX has announced development of the Falcon family of mostly reusable two stage, RP-1 kerosene/liquid oxygen launch vehicles, including the [[Falcon I]] rocket with one lower stage engine, one upper stage engine and the larger [[Falcon V]] rocket with five lower stage engines, two upper stage engines.  The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for September, 2004, with a United States [[Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency]] (DARPA) [[Tacsat]] payload.  The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for late 2005 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]].  Musk co-founded two previous startup companies:  Zip2, a leader in the online news publishing software business; and [[PayPal]], a leader in the online funds transfer business.  Zip2 was sold in 1999 to the computer hardware and services company [[Compaq]] for US$341 million in cash and stock options [http://h18020.www1.hp.com/corporate/1999ar/nf/fin_notes2.html] and PayPal was sold in 2002 to online auctioneer [[eBay]] for US$1.5 billion in stock.[http://www.shareholder.com/Common/Edgar/1065088/891618-03-1538/03-00.pdf]  Musk funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. In an interview Musk has stated: "I don’t want to say specific numbers. But I’ve certainly spent more than I expected to spend. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will."

* [http://www.space.com/spacenews/businessmonday_040816.html Privately Funded Falcon-1 Rocket Nears First Flight]
* [http://www.hobbyspace.com/AAdmin/archive/Interviews/Systems/ElonMusk.html Interview with Elon Musk about SpaceX]

Space Exploration Technologies Corporation ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to space "ultimately by a factor of ten".

SpaceX has announced development of the Falcon family of mostly reusable two stage, RP-1 kerosene/liquid oxygen launch vehicles, including the [[Falcon I]] rocket with one lower stage engine, one upper stage engine and the larger [[Falcon V]] rocket with five lower stage engines, one upper stage engine.  The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for March, 2005, with a United States [[Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency]] (DARPA) [[Tacsat]] payload.  The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for the second quarter of 2006 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade.  SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before January 10, 2010.  SpaceX will use its Falcon V vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]].  Musk co-founded two previous startup companies:  Zip2, a leader in the online news publishing software business; and [[PayPal]], a leader in the online funds transfer business.  Zip2 was sold in 1999 to the computer hardware and services company [[Compaq]] for US$341 million in cash and stock options [http://h18020.www1.hp.com/corporate/1999ar/nf/fin_notes2.html] and PayPal was sold in 2002 to online auctioneer [[eBay]] for US$1.5 billion in stock.[http://www.shareholder.com/Common/Edgar/1065088/891618-03-1538/03-00.pdf]  Musk funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. In an interview Musk has stated: "I don’t want to say specific numbers. But I’ve certainly spent more than I expected to spend. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will."

Space Exploration Technologies Corporation ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to space "ultimately by a factor of ten".

SpaceX has announced development of the Falcon family of mostly reusable two stage, RP-1 kerosene/liquid oxygen launch vehicles, including the [[Falcon I]] rocket with one lower stage engine, one upper stage engine and the larger [[Falcon V]] rocket with five lower stage engines, one upper stage engine.  The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for late Summer 2005, with a United States [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload.  The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for the second quarter of 2006 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]].  Musk co-founded two previous startup companies:  [[Zip2]], a leader in the online news publishing software business; and [[PayPal]], a leader in the online funds transfer business.  Zip2 was sold in 1999 to the computer hardware and services company [[Compaq]] for US$341 million in cash and stock options [http://h18020.www1.hp.com/corporate/1999ar/nf/fin_notes2.html] and PayPal was sold in 2002 to online auctioneer [[eBay]] for US$1.5 billion in stock.[http://www.shareholder.com/Common/Edgar/1065088/891618-03-1538/03-00.pdf]  Musk funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. In an interview Musk has stated: "I don’t want to say specific numbers. But I’ve certainly spent more than I expected to spend. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will." 

On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded a $100 million [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]] [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791].

== Future development ==

While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade.  SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before January 10, 2010.  SpaceX will use its Falcon V vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]

Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin engine (rocket)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future.  The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big F--king Rocket" (BFR), presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game.  In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound or less is very achievable."

The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten".

The larger [[Falcon V]] rocket has five lower stage engines and one upper stage engine.  

The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for late September 2005, with a United States [[DARPA]] payload. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload.  

The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for the second quarter of 2006 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]]. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will."

On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded a $100 million [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]] [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791].

As of June 8, 2005, the company has around 130 employees.

== Future development ==
[[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|280px|The first [[Falcon I]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]]

While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade.  SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before January 10, 2010.  SpaceX will use its Falcon V vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]

Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin engine (rocket)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future.  The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big Fucking Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game.  In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound or less is very achievable."

The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten".

SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1]] [[kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s, including the [[Falcon I]] [[rocket]] with one lower stage engine and one upper stage engine. The larger [[Falcon V]] rocket has five lower stage engines and one upper stage engine.  

The maiden launch of the Falcon I is scheduled for late September 2005, with a United States [[DARPA]] payload. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload.  

The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for the fourth quarter of 2006 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload.

SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]]. Musk funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. In an interview Musk has stated: "I don’t want to say specific numbers. But I’ve certainly spent more than I expected to spend. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will." Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he has also stated, "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business."

On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded a $100 million [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]] [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791].

As of June 8, 2005, the company has around 130 employees.  This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve: he sees traditional rocket-producers as providing rockets at a bloated price and supporting an unnecessary beauracracy - something he intends to cut out of the manufacture of future rockets.

== Future development ==
[[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|280px|The first [[Falcon I]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]]

While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade.  SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before January 10, 2010.  SpaceX will use its Falcon V vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]

Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin engine (rocket)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future.  The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big Fucking Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game.  In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound or less is very achievable."

SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] on Sept. 8, 2005, the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon V which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an  [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV]] and [[Atlas V]]. Like the Falcon V, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon V was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks.

{{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 130 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} [[Image:SpaceX-FalconRocketFamily.png|thumb|right|276px|The Falcon family]] The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon I]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon V]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and funds SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. In an interview Musk has stated: "I don’t want to say specific numbers. But I’ve certainly spent more than I expected to spend. On Falcon I alone I’m unlikely to recoup my investment, but with Falcon V and Falcon I, together I think that I probably will." Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he has also stated, "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of June 8, 2005, the company has around 130 employees. This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve: he sees traditional rocket-producers as providing rockets at a bloated price and supporting an unnecessary bureaucracy - something he intends to cut out of the manufacture of future rockets.

[[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 6.7 |align="right"| 18 |align="right"| 27 |align="right"| 35 |align="right"| 51 |align="right"| 78 |} ==Upcoming launches== The maiden flight of the Falcon I is scheduled for October 31, 2005, with a United States [[DARPA]] payload launched from [[Omelek Island]] . The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W. The maiden launch of the Falcon V is scheduled for the fourth quarter of 2006 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload. On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded a $100 million [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]] [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == [[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|276px|The first [[Falcon I]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]] While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before January 10, 2010. SpaceX will use its Falcon V vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html] Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin engine (rocket)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big Fucking Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] on Sept. 8, 2005, the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon V which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket]]. Like the Falcon V, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon V was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.discover.com/issues/sep-05/cover/ Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine, subscription-only cover article, September 2005) [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]]

On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded a $100 million [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]] [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == [[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|276px|The first [[Falcon 1]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]] While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 [$8,800/kg to $2,900/kg] between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] on [[September 8]], [[2005]], the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket]]. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust, definitely earning the title of BFTS! == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.discover.com/issues/sep-05/cover/ Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine, subscription-only cover article, September 2005) [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]]

Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust, definitely earning the title of BFTS! == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewsr.html?pid=18460 Full Summons and Complaint by SpaceX against Boeing Company and Lockheed Martin Corporation] (October 23, 2005) === Articles === * [http://spacex.com/media42.php Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine cover article, September 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]]

{{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 130 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon 1]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon 5]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by Chief Executive Officer [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and so far has invested about $100 million in SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he also has stated: "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of November, 2005, the company has approximately 160 employees.[http://www.thespacereview.com/article/497/1] This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve. He sees other rocket manufacturers as providing space launch services at an unreasonably high price, due to support of an unnecessary bureaucracy.

[[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 6.7 |align="right"| 18 |align="right"| 27 |align="right"| 35 |align="right"| 51 |align="right"| 78 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Low Earth orbit|LEO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 11,754 |align="right"| 4,390 |align="right"| 2,903 |align="right"| 4,023 |align="right"| 3,091 |align="right"| 3,152 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Geostationary transfer orbit|GTO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| -- |align="right"| 17,143 |align="right"| 7,941 |align="right"| 11,290 |align="right"| 7,969 |align="right"| 8,083 |} ==Upcoming launches== The maiden flight of the Falcon 1 is scheduled for February 2006 at the earliest[http://www.spacedaily.com/news/launchers-05zzzy.html], with a United States [[DARPA]] payload launched from [[Omelek Island]] in the [[Kwajalein Atoll]]. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for the second quarter of 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded an [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]], which could allow the Air Force to purchase up to $100 million worth of launches from the company [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == [[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|276px|The first [[Falcon 1]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]] While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before [[January 10]], [[2010]]. SpaceX will use its Falcon 5 vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html] Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin engine (rocket)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big Fucking Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 [$8,800/kg to $2,900/kg] between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket]]. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust, definitely earning the title of BFTS! == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewsr.html?pid=18460 Full Summons and Complaint by SpaceX against Boeing Company and Lockheed Martin Corporation] (October 23, 2005) === Articles === * [http://thespacereview.com/article/497/1 Big plans for SpaceX] (The Space Review, November 14, 2005) * [http://spacex.com/media42.php Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine cover article, September 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] (The Space Show) [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]]

[[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 6.7 |align="right"| 18 |align="right"| 27 |align="right"| 35 |align="right"| 51 |align="right"| 78 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Low Earth orbit|LEO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 11,754 |align="right"| 4,390 |align="right"| 2,903 |align="right"| 4,023 |align="right"| 3,091 |align="right"| 3,152 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Geostationary transfer orbit|GTO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| -- |align="right"| 17,143 |align="right"| 7,941 |align="right"| 11,290 |align="right"| 7,969 |align="right"| 8,083 |} ==Upcoming launches== [[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|246px|The first [[Falcon 1]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]] The maiden flight of the Falcon 1 is to be scheduled sometime in December 2005, with a United States [[DARPA]] payload launched from [[Omelek Island]] in the [[Kwajalein Atoll]]. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W. Weather and ground-related holds have prevented the lift-off on Saturday, November 26. Issues with the liquid oxygen and helium ground supplies inventories at the pad will required replenishment from [[Hawaii]]. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for the second quarter of 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. On May 2, 2005, the company announced that it had been awarded an [[IDIQ]] (indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]], which could allow the Air Force to purchase up to $100 million worth of launches from the company [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before [[January 10]], [[2010]]. SpaceX will use its Falcon 5 vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html] Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin (rocket engine)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big Fucking Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 [$8,800/kg to $2,900/kg] between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV rocket|Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket|Atlas V]] rockets. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust. == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewsr.html?pid=18460 Full Summons and Complaint by SpaceX against Boeing Company and Lockheed Martin Corporation] (October 23, 2005) * [http://michaelbelfiore.com/blog/2005/11/spacex-prelaunch-conference.html Notes from SpaceX pre-launch conference] (Michael Belfiore, November 18, 2005) === Articles === * [http://thespacereview.com/article/497/1 Big plans for SpaceX] (The Space Review, November 14, 2005) * [http://spacex.com/media42.php Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine cover article, September 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] (The Space Show) [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]]

{{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 160 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo, California|El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon 1]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon 5]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by CEO/CTO [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and so far has invested about $100 million in SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he also has stated: "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of November, 2005, the company has approximately 160 employees.[http://www.thespacereview.com/article/497/1] Its launch crew in the Marshall Islands has 25 people, with 6 people in mission control. This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve. He sees other rocket manufacturers as providing space launch services at an unreasonably high price, due to support of an unnecessary bureaucracy. ==Launcher versions== [[Image:SpaceX-FalconRocketFamily.png|thumb|right|246px|The Falcon family]] [[Image:SpaceX_falcon_in_warehouse.jpg|thumb|right|246px|[[Falcon 1]] prototype.]] {| border="2" cellpadding="4" cellspacing="0" style="margin: 1em 1em 1em 0; background: #f9f9f9; border: 1px #aaa solid; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 95%;" |----- bgcolor="#FFDEAD" ! Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. In January 2005, SpaceX bought a 10% stake in [[Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd]]. On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV rocket|Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket|Atlas V]] rockets. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust. == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] * [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewsr.html?pid=18460 Full Summons and Complaint by SpaceX against Boeing Company and Lockheed Martin Corporation] (October 23, 2005) * [http://michaelbelfiore.com/blog/2005/11/spacex-prelaunch-conference.html Notes from SpaceX pre-launch conference] (Michael Belfiore, November 18, 2005) * [http://kwajrockets.blogspot.com/ ''Kwajalein Atoll and Rockets''] (blog, on-site pictures and reporting.) === Articles === * [http://thespacereview.com/article/497/1 Big plans for SpaceX] (The Space Review, November 14, 2005) * [http://spacex.com/media42.php Shooting the Moon] (Discover Magazine cover article, September 2005) * [http://www.sciencentral.com/articles/view.php3?article_id=218392620&cat=2_1 Space X, with video interviews] (ScienCentral News, August 12, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/missionlaunches/050812_spacex_island.html SpaceX Private Rocket Shifts to Island Launch] (Space.com, August 12, 2005) * [http://spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050527frf/ Privately-made Falcon 1 rocket roars on the pad] (Spaceflight Now, May 27, 2005) * [http://www.space.com/adastra/050523_musk_nss.html Private rocketeer looks to August flight] (Space.com, May 23, 2005) * [http://www.santamariatimes.com/articles/2005/04/27/news/local/news04.txt Small firm fired up to test new rocket] (Santa Maria Times, April 27, 2005) * [http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html Hondas in Space] (Fast Company, February 2005) * [http://www.spaceflightnow.com/falcon/050120spacex/ SpaceX starting small as it dreams of grand plans] (Spaceflight Now, January 20, 2005) * [http://www.thespaceshow.com/guest.asp?q=15 Broadcast interviews with Elon Musk about SpaceX] (The Space Show) [[Category:Aerospace companies]] [[Category:Private spaceflight]] [[de:SpaceX]] [[ru:SpaceX]]

{{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 160 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo, California|El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon 1]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon 5]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by CEO/CTO [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and so far has invested about $100 million in SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he also has stated: "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of November, 2005, the company has approximately 160 employees.[http://www.thespacereview.com/article/497/1] Its launch crew in the Marshall Islands has 25 people, with 6 people in mission control. This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve. He sees other rocket manufacturers as providing space launch services at an unreasonably high price, due to support of an unnecessary bureaucracy. ==Launcher versions== [[Image:SpaceX-FalconRocketFamily.png|thumb|right|246px|The Falcon family]] [[Image:SpaceX_falcon_in_warehouse.jpg|thumb|right|246px|[[Falcon 1]] prototype.]] {| border="2" cellpadding="4" cellspacing="0" style="margin: 1em 1em 1em 0; background: #f9f9f9; border: 1px #aaa solid; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 95%;" |----- bgcolor="#FFDEAD" ! The payload, [[FalconSAT-2]], will study [[Plasma (physics)|plasma]] in the [[upper atmosphere]]. This will be the third launch attempt for the Falcon 1. Previously, weather and ground-related holds scrubbed an earlier launch attempt on [[November 26]], [[2005]], followed by another scrubbed launch attempt on [[December 19]], [[2005]], when a faulty valve caused the first stage kerosene tank to deform during an unfueling maneuver. The launch tower has since been redesigned to reduce LOX boiloff and avoid wind-related holds. Further static testing before a launch scheduled for February 10th led to another unspecified delay while data from the run-up are analyzed. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W, in early 2006. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for late 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. On [[May 2]], [[2005]], the company announced that it had been awarded an [[Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity]] (IDIQ) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]], which could allow the Air Force to purchase up to $100 million worth of launches from the company [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately-financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before [[January 10]], [[2010]]. SpaceX will use its Falcon 5 vehicle to pursue the prize.[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html] Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin (rocket engine)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big F*****g Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 [$8,800/kg to $2,900/kg] between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. In January 2005, SpaceX bought a 10% stake in [[Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd]]. On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV rocket|Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket|Atlas V]] rockets. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big Fucking Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 metric tons force (15 MN) of thrust. == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] The payload, [[FalconSAT-2]], will study [[Plasma (physics)|plasma]] in the [[upper atmosphere]]. It will be preceded by another static fire, to check-out launch procedures, tentatively scheduled for around March 17th. In the time before the launch, the rocket's second stage tank will be replaced following the discovery of a small leak in the original. This will be the third launch attempt for the Falcon 1. Previously, weather and ground-related holds scrubbed an earlier launch attempt on [[November 26]], [[2005]], followed by another scrubbed launch attempt on [[December 19]], [[2005]], when a faulty valve caused the first stage kerosene tank to deform during an unfueling maneuver. The launch tower has since been redesigned to reduce LOX boiloff and avoid wind-related holds. Further static testing before a launch scheduled for February 10th led to another unspecified delay while data from the run-up are analyzed. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W, in early 2006. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for late 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. On [[May 2]], [[2005]], the company announced that it had been awarded an [[Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity]] (IDIQ) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]], which could allow the Air Force to purchase up to $100 million worth of launches from the company [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. == Future development == === Manned orbital spaceflight: SpaceX ''Dragon'' === While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before [[January 10]], [[2010]]. The company has also expressed an interest in competing for crew and cargo resupply contracts to the [[International Space Station]] under the NASA Commercial Orbital Transportation Services (COTS) program. More details of the manned program were released on [[March 6]], [[2006]][http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewnews.html?id=1095]. The [[SpaceX Dragon]] is a conventional blunt-cone ballistic capsule, capable of carrying 7 people or a mixture of personnel and cargo, to and from low earth orbit. The nosecone of the vehicle has a hinged cap which opens to reveal a standard [[International Space Station|ISS]] Common Berthing Mechanism, which allows the Dragon to dock to the US segment of the ISS. The Dragon capsule will be launched atop a Falcon 9 vehicle. === Heavy Lift Launchers === On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 tonnes force (15 MN) of thrust. Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin (rocket engine)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big F*****g Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. {{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 160 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo, California|El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon 1]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon 5]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by CEO/CTO [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and so far has invested about $100 million in SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he also has stated: "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of November, 2005, the company has approximately 160 employees.[http://www.thespacereview.com/article/497/1] Its launch crew in the Marshall Islands has 25 people, with 6 people in mission control. This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve. He sees other rocket manufacturers as providing space launch services at an unreasonably high price, due to support of an unnecessary bureaucracy. ==Launcher versions== [[Image:SpaceX-FalconRocketFamily.png|thumb|right|246px|The Falcon family]] [[Image:SpaceX_falcon_in_warehouse.jpg|thumb|right|246px|[[Falcon 1]] prototype.]] {| border="2" cellpadding="4" cellspacing="0" style="margin: 1em 1em 1em 0; background: #f9f9f9; border: 1px #aaa solid; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 95%;" |----- bgcolor="#FFDEAD" ! [[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 6.7 |align="right"| 18 |align="right"| 27 |align="right"| 35 |align="right"| 51 |align="right"| 78 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Low Earth orbit|LEO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| 11,754 |align="right"| 4,390 |align="right"| 2,903 |align="right"| 4,023 |align="right"| 3,091 |align="right"| 3,152 |- | '''Price/Kg ([[Geostationary transfer orbit|GTO]]) ([[Dollar|$]])''' |align="right"| -- |align="right"| 17,143 |align="right"| 7,941 |align="right"| 11,290 |align="right"| 7,969 |align="right"| 8,083 |} ==Upcoming launches== [[Image:SpaceX Falcon verticle on the launch pad.jpg|thumb|right|246px|The first [[Falcon 1]] at Space Launch Complex - Three West (SLC-3W), Vandenberg Airforce Base.]] The launch window for the maiden flight of the Falcon 1 is March 20th-25th [[2006]], with a United States [[DARPA]] payload launched from [[Omelek Island]] in the [[Kwajalein Atoll]]. The payload, [[FalconSAT-2]], will study [[Plasma (physics)|plasma]] in the [[upper atmosphere]]. It will be preceded by another static fire, to check-out launch procedures, tentatively scheduled for around March 17th. In the time before the launch, the rocket's second stage tank will be replaced following the discovery of a small leak in the original. This will be the third launch attempt for the Falcon 1. Previously, weather and ground-related holds scrubbed an earlier launch attempt on [[November 26]], [[2005]], followed by another scrubbed launch attempt on [[December 19]], [[2005]], when a faulty valve caused the first stage kerosene tank to deform during an unfueling maneuver. The launch tower has since been redesigned to reduce LOX boiloff and avoid wind-related holds. Further static testing before a launch scheduled for February 10th led to another unspecified delay. Testing and equipment modificaitons were completed and launch occured at 2:30 PM PST (22:30 UTC), however the rocket was destroyed shortly after launch. The second launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] [[Optical Sciences Division]] [[Tacsat]] payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W, in early 2006. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for late 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. On [[May 2]], [[2005]], the company announced that it had been awarded an [[Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity]] (IDIQ) contract for Responsive Small Spacelift (RSS) launch services by the [[United States Air Force]], which could allow the Air Force to purchase up to $100 million worth of launches from the company [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewpr.html?pid=16791]. ===Maiden Flight=== The Falcon 1 maiden flight was originally scheduled for 1:00 PM PST (4:00 PM EST, 9:00 PM GMT) on 24 March from Kwajalien. A live webcast from various pad cameras, with mission control voiceover was provided. There was an unplanned hold of about 90 minutes. The vehicle launched at 5:30 PM EST on 24 March. The webcam showed water dump, ignition, and pad liftoff, and the view switched to an onboard camera, with the atoll clearly receding in the background. about 30 seconds into the flight the webcam went dead. No further information is available yet. == Future development == === Manned orbital spaceflight: SpaceX ''Dragon'' === While currently focused on the launch of unmanned spacecraft, SpaceX has announced that it plans to pursue a manned commercial space program through the end of the decade[http://www.wired.com/news/space/0,2697,66308,00.html]. SpaceX seeks to win [[America's Space Prize]], which will award $50 million to the first U.S. company that launches at least 5 astronauts on a privately financed and developed reusable spacecraft to [[Low Earth Orbit]] twice within 60 days before [[January 10]], [[2010]]. The company has also expressed an interest in competing for crew and cargo resupply contracts to the [[International Space Station]] under the NASA [[Commercial Orbital Transportation Services]] (COTS) program. More details of the manned program were released on [[March 6]], [[2006]][http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewnews.html?id=1095]. The [[SpaceX Dragon]] is a conventional blunt-cone ballistic capsule, capable of carrying 7 people or a mixture of personnel and cargo, to and from low earth orbit. The nosecone of the vehicle has a hinged cap which opens to reveal a standard [[International Space Station|ISS]] Common Berthing Mechanism, which allows the Dragon to dock to the US segment of the ISS. The Dragon capsule will be launched atop a Falcon 9 vehicle. === Heavy Lift Launchers === On [[September 8]], [[2005]], SpaceX [http://www.spacex.com/index.html?section=media&content=http%3A//www.spacex.com/press18.php announced] the development of the [[Falcon 9]], an upgrade to the Falcon 5 which would have nine Merlin engines in its first stage [http://selenianboondocks.blogspot.com/2005/08/falcon-ix.html]. The design is an [[EELV]] class vehicle, intended to compete with the [[Delta IV rocket|Delta IV]] and the [[Atlas V rocket|Atlas V]] rockets. Like the Falcon 5, all stages will be designed to be reusable. Also the configuration of Falcon 5 was changed, it is now a downscaled Falcon 9 with five Merlin engines in its first stage and not completely fueled tanks. According to the SpaceX updates page, they have been working on the test stand for the Falcon 9 called the BFTS, probably standing for "Big F*****g Test Stand". This test stand will need to provide for the 350 [[kilogram-force|metric tons force]] (3.4 [[meganewton|MN]]) of thrust that the 9 Merlin 1Bs are capable of delivering. The test stand has been built to withstand 1500 tonnes force (15 MN) of thrust. Also, SpaceX has announced plans for the development of the [[Merlin (rocket engine)|Merlin]] 2 engine, a scale version of a larger [[F-1]]-class engine to be developed in the future. The company is rumored to be working on a very large rocket to accompany the F-1-class engine, referred to in-house as the "Big F*****g Rocket" (BFR)[http://www.fastcompany.com/magazine/91/honda.html], presumably in reference to the [[BFG9000]] weapon in the [[Doom]] video game. In the past Musk has stated, "Long term plans call for development of a heavy lift product and even a super-heavy, if there is customer demand. We expect that each size increase would result in a meaningful decrease in cost per pound to orbit. For example, dollar cost per pound to orbit dropped from $4,000 to $1,300 [$8,800/kg to $2,900/kg] between Falcon 1 and Falcon 5. Ultimately, I believe $500 per pound [$1,100/kg] or less is very achievable." On other occasions, Musk has stated that he expects to be able to offer a price of $1000 per kilogram by 2010. === Satellite systems === In January 2005, SpaceX bought a 10% stake in [[Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd]]. == External links == {{Commons|Falcon (rocket)|Falcon rockets}} * [http://www.spacex.com/ SpaceX official website] ** [http://www.spacex.com/MAIDENFLIGHTB27.pdf Maiden flight slide presentation] ([[Portable Document Format|pdf]]) * [http://www.spaceref.com/news/viewsr.html?pid=18460 Full Summons and Complaint by SpaceX against Boeing Company and Lockheed Martin Corporation] (October 23, 2005) * [http://michaelbelfiore.com/blog/2005/11/spacex-prelaunch-conference.html Notes from SpaceX pre-launch conference] (Michael Belfiore, November 18, 2005) * [http://kwajrockets.blogspot.com/ ''Kwajalein Atoll and Rockets''] (Candid and highly unofficial blog by Elon Musk's brother Kimbal, with on-site pictures and reporting.) === Articles === * [http://thespacereview.com/article/567/1 A tour/interview of SpaceX] (The Space Review, February 27 - The final launch window for the maiden flight of the Falcon 1 was March 20th-25th [[2006]], with a United States [[DARPA]] payload launched from [[Omelek Island]] in the [[Kwajalein Atoll]]. The payload, [[FalconSAT-2]], was intended to study [[Plasma (physics)|plasma]] in the [[upper atmosphere]]. It was preceded by static firings, to check-out launch procedures, one which happened March 17th, followed by an additional one on 22 March. In the time before the launch, the rocket's second stage tank was be replaced following the discovery of a small leak in the original. This was the third launch attempt for the Falcon 1. Previously, weather and ground-related holds scrubbed the first launch attempt on [[November 26]], [[2005]], followed by another scrubbed launch attempt on [[December 19]], [[2005]], when a faulty valve caused the first stage kerosene tank to deform during an unfueling maneuver. The launch tower was since redesigned to reduce LOX boiloff and avoid wind-related holds. Further static testing before a launch scheduled for February 10th led to another unspecified delay. Testing and equipment modificat0ons were completed and launch occured at 2:30 PM PST (22:30 UTC) on March 24th, however the rocket and payload was destroyed shortly after launch. The Falcon 1 maiden flight was originally scheduled for 1:00 PM PST (4:00 PM EST, 9:00 PM GMT) on 24 March from Kwajalien. A live webcast from various pad cameras, with mission control voiceover was provided. There was an unplanned hold of about 90 minutes. The vehicle launched at 5:30 PM EST on 24 March. The webcam showed water dump, ignition, and pad liftoff, and the view switched to an onboard camera, with the atoll clearly receding in the background. about 30 seconds into the flight the webcam went dead. SpaceX reported that the rocket and its satellite were destroyed during the ascent. "We did lose the vehicle," said Gwynne Shotwell, the company's vice president for business development to reporters via a telephone link. "Clearly this is a setback," she said, "but we're in this for the long haul." No further information is available. ===Upcoming launches=== The second Falcon 1 launch will loft a [[Naval Research Laboratory]] Optical Sciences Division Tacsat payload from [[Vandenberg Air Force Base]], Pad 3W, was originally scheduled for early 2006, but presumably will be pushed back by several months according to MSNBC.com. This flight was originally scheduled to carry a secondary payload, arranged through Texas-based [[Space Services]] Inc.: memorial capsules containing the cremated remains of 187 people, including [[Project Mercury]] astronaut [[Gordon Cooper]] and ''[[Star Trek]]'' actor [[James Doohan]]. The maiden launch of the Falcon 9 is scheduled for late 2007 with a US Government payload followed by a launch in the first quarter of 2008 with a [[Bigelow Aerospace]] [[Genesis Pathfinder]] expandable space station module payload[http://www.spacex.com/falcon_overview.php]. {{Infobox_Company | company_name = SpaceX | company_logo = [[Image:Spacex_logo.gif|center|]] | company_type = [[Private company|Private]] | company_slogan = "Revolutionizing access to space" (?) | foundation = 2002 | location = [[El Segundo, California]] | key_people = [[Elon Musk]]: [[Chief executive officer|CEO]] and [[Chief technical officer|Chief Technology Officer]] | num_employees = 160 | industry = [[Aerospace engineering|Aerospace]] | products = [[orbital_spaceflight|Orbital]] [[rocket launch]] | revenue = unknown| homepage = [http://www.spacex.com/ www.spacex.com] }} The '''Space Exploration Technologies Corporation''' ('''SpaceX''') is an [[El Segundo, California|El Segundo]], [[California]] [[space transportation]] [[startup company]] whose stated goal is to improve the cost and reliability of access to [[outer space|space]] "ultimately by a factor of ten". SpaceX is developing a family of mostly reusable [[Two stage to orbit|two stage]], [[RP-1|kerosene]]/[[liquid oxygen]] [[rocket launch|launch vehicle]]s. The [[Falcon 1]] is designed with a reusable lower stage, while the larger [[Falcon 5]] and [[Falcon 9]] will be the world's first fully reusable launchers and have multiple first stage [[Spacecraft propulsion|engines]]. ==Background== SpaceX was founded in June, 2002, by CEO/CTO [[Elon Musk]]. Musk co-founded two previous [[startup company|startup companies]]: [[Zip2]] and [[PayPal]], and so far has invested about $100 million in SpaceX from the fortune gained through these sales. Although Musk has stated that he could financially handle a couple of early launch failures, he also has stated: "If we have three consecutive failures... it’s not clear to me that we know what we’re doing and maybe we should go out of business." As of November, 2005, the company has approximately 160 employees.[http://www.thespacereview.com/article/497/1] Its launch crew in the Marshall Islands has 25 people, with 6 people in mission control. This small number of employees, when compared to other companies that produce similar-sized rockets, is part of the cost-cutting that Musk is trying to achieve. He sees other rocket manufacturers as providing space launch services at an unreasonably high price, due to support of an unnecessary bureaucracy. ==Launcher versions== [[Image:SpaceX-FalconRocketFamily.png|thumb|right|246px|The Falcon family]] [[Image:SpaceX_falcon_in_warehouse.jpg|thumb|right|246px|[[Falcon 1]] prototype.]] {| border="2" cellpadding="4" cellspacing="0" style="margin: 1em 1em 1em 0; background: #f9f9f9; border: 1px #aaa solid; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 95%;" |----- bgcolor="#FFDEAD" !
